channel.title: Psychology Today – Latest
channel.description: Latest articles from Psychology Today (full content)

item.title: 6 Ways Schools Undermine Autistic Students' Self-Advocacy
item.description:
Neurodiversity

6 Ways Schools Undermine Autistic Students' Self-Advocacy

A new study explored autistic self-advocacy but found systems-level ableism.

Posted March 19, 2026

Key points
  • Autistic and neurodivergent researchers have released a new study on autistic experiences at school.
  • Autistic self-advocacy at school faces barriers that require structural and systemic solutions.
  • Barriers include lack of knowledge about autism, stereotyping, and disbelieving student accounts.
Source: hayriyenur/ Pexels

A new study published in Autism delivers findings that validate what many autistic self-advocates have felt in their bones: Autistic students struggle to advocate for themselves in school and to request and obtain the support they need. Regardless of individual self-advocacy skills, systemic factors keep them back. Not just individual teachers or principals, but the entire structures of meaning, mattering, and management make the autistic experience difficult.

An autistic and neurodivergent team—Nicole Nadwodny, Ben VanHook, Brady Esham, Luna N Larsen, Sarah Levinson, and Abbey Eisenhower—set out to examine how K–12 school experiences shaped the self-advocacy of autistic students. In a community-focused design, 19 autistic adolescents and adults in the US participated in 90-minute semi-structured interviews about their K–12 school experiences. Key findings pointed to common systemic and structural issues experienced by participants. The analysis distilled common barriers to six factors: erasure, conformity, isolation, oppression, hidden expectations, and authority.

The Core Finding: It’s the System

The research team expected to learn which conditions helped autistic students advocate for themselves. Instead, participants kept focusing the conversation on a fundamental obstacle: Their schools were not structurally conducive to autistic self-advocacy.

The core theme that emerged from the analysis was that individual educators, even well-meaning ones, cannot dismantle ableism baked into institutional foundations. Participants described encountering barriers not so much from bad actors but from the ordinary operation of school systems.

6 Obstacles To Autistic Students' Self-Advocacy

The analysis of participants' reports suggested that barriers clustered into six categories.

  • Erasure. Autism was rarely acknowledged or discussed in school. When it was, the narratives were deficit-framed and stereotyped, like it was a "deadly disease, which once you are diagnosed you can never be anybody in the future.” Even the support took the form of "Treat your peers with kindness even if they are weird" lesson. The absence of accurate information about autism meant that many participants internalized ableism and felt that they themselves, or their autistic traits, were the problem, which greatly limited their self-advocacy.
  • Conformity. Schools enforced neurotypical behavioral norms through rigidity, social pressure, and punishment. One student recalled being marked as absent because she had been "too long" in the bathroom, crying. Students were taught to blend in, and even academically positive behaviors, like reading ahead in a textbook, had negative consequences when they deviated from the norm. As a result, fear of expressing themselves became another obstacle to self-advocacy.
  • Isolation. Participants described both physical separation (through special-education placements) and social isolation. Public singling-out taught neurotypical peers exactly who was the "other." One student reported that a teacher joked: “Okay class, guess who in this class wrote two pages for an answer that required one sentence?" Isolation also prevented some of the participants from forming a community that might have supported both well-being and self-advocacy.
  • Oppression. For participants with multiple marginalized identities, school ableism did not operate in isolation. It compounded with other forms of bias. One participant noted, “if you’re autistic but you’re like, really beautiful then it might be like ‘oh, she’s just quirky,’ but when you’re seen as fat and ugly . . . then it’s like, you know, unacceptable.”
  • Hidden expectations. Schools assumed students knew the unspoken rules, the social codebook, the practical life skills, the unwritten behavioral contracts, without ever teaching them. When students didn't understand the expectations or how they came across, they weren't taught; they were punished. One participant remembered "being screamed at for like, insubordination . . . or having an attitude" while feeling like "I honestly have no clue what you’re talking about.” Participants described asking for clarifications and being ignored or met with visible irritation, which made further self-advocacy even more difficult.
  • Authority. Power hierarchies in schools left students with little recourse or voice. When autistic students tried to advocate for themselves, they were frequently disbelieved or blamed for being bullied. One participant recalled a meeting with the principal, vice principal, guidance counselor, and gym teacher, all gathered to address the fact that he was being physically beaten nearly every day. They concluded that being "too vulnerable" was the student's fault. Victim-blaming and being targeted for speaking out presented yet another obstacle for self-advocates.
article continues after advertisement

How to Support Autistic Students' Self-Advocacy

The study's implications for practice are direct. School-based interventions for autistic students have often focused on teaching self-advocacy skills to individuals. That work has value, but this study provides a structural corrective. Teaching students to speak up means little if the institution punishes them for doing so.

The study’s recommendations are organized around the same six structural barriers its participants identified.

  • On erasure: Autism needs to be visible in schools. Not as tragedy, not as quirk, and not as the subject of a kindness lesson that ends with “be nice to the weird kids," but visible as a legitimate form of human neurological variation with its own history, culture, and expertise. The authors recommend bringing in neurodivergent professional development speakers, consulting neurodivergent community members when designing special education policy, and piloting autism-affirming classroom resources. We cannot expect students to develop a positive identity around something their school treats as unspeakable.
  • On conformity: Instead of enforcing conformity, encourage autistic leadership development. Leadership skills built into the curriculum can support participation and celebration of autistic differences.
  • On isolation: Community is not a nice-to-have. Finding other people like you makes self-advocacy imaginable. Schools can actively build that through sensory-accessible school-wide events, cross-classroom community activities, and student-led affinity groups in which autistic students can find each other without it being a clinical intervention. Representation on the faculty side matters too.
  • On oppression: To combat ableist biases, schools may incorporate disabled characters and disabled artists across curricula, partner with local disability advocacy organizations, and form anti-ableism committees.
  • On hidden expectations: Explicit instruction on social expectations, practical life skills, and what “attitude” means when a teacher says you have one can make the hidden curriculum visible before students are punished for not knowing it.
  • On authority: The authors recommend training educators to shift the focus from policing visible behavior to understanding the needs driving it, and to explicitly create space for students to contribute their own ideas for solving their problems.

Restoring Epistemic Justice

This study contributed to combating epistemic injustice by both serving as an example of neurodivergent-led research and calling for expanded participation by autistic voices. Autistic researchers analyzing the autistic experience is an act of epistemic repair. It returns interpretive authority to the people whose interpretations have historically been overridden, discounted, and pathologized.

What this study asks of schools, and what epistemic justice demands more broadly, is the harder and more honest alternative: to treat the autistic canary’s signal as data. Instead of asking “How do we get this student to fit in better in this system,” ask “What is this student’s distress telling us about the system itself?”

article continues after advertisement

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Ludmila N. Praslova, Ph.D.
